title: Extinction Risk Estimation
text: Estimates demographic parameters extinction probabilities time series population sizes based Wiener-drift process model. Implements w-z method (Hakoyama, 2025) improve accuracy confidence intervals conventional approaches

title: Extinction Risk Estimation
code: library(extr) dat <- data.frame(Time = c(1959, 1960, 1961, 1962, 1963, 1964, 1965, 1966, 1967, 1968, 1969, 1970, 1971, 1972, 1973, 1974, 1975, 1976, 1977, 1978, 1979, 1980, 1981, 1982, 1983, 1984, 1985, 1986, 1987), Population = c(44, 47, 46, 44, 46, 45, 46, 40, 39, 39, 42, 44, 41, 40, 33, 36, 34, 39, 35, 34, 38, 36, 37, 41, 39, 51, 47, 57, 47)) ext_di(dat, th = 100) #> --- Estimates --- #>                                                       Estimate #> Probability of decline to 1 within 100 years (MLE): 9.4128e-05 #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   0.0023556 #> Variance (MLE):                                        0.01087 #> Unbiased variance:                                    0.011273 #> AIC for the distribution of N:                          165.06 #>                                                                        CI #> Probability of decline to 1 within 100 years (MLE):  (1.4586e-13, 0.5653) #> Growth rate (MLE):                                  (-0.038814, 0.043525) #> Variance (MLE):                                     (0.0070464, 0.020885) #> Unbiased variance:                                                      - #> AIC for the distribution of N:                                          - #>  #> --- Data Summary --- #>                               Value #> Current population size, nq:     47 #> xd = ln(nq / ne):            3.8501 #> Sample size, q + 1:              29 #>  #> --- Input Parameters --- #>                                                         Parameter #> Time unit:                                                  years #> Extinction threshold of population size, ne:                    1 #> Time window for extinction risk evaluation (years), th:       100 #> Significance level, alpha:                                   0.05 ext_di(dat, th = 100, ne = 10) #> --- Estimates --- #>                                                       Estimate #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  0.096852 #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   0.0023556 #> Variance (MLE):                                        0.01087 #> Unbiased variance:                                    0.011273 #> AIC for the distribution of N:                          165.06 #>                                                                         CI #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  (1.0698e-05, 0.9898) #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   (-0.038814, 0.043525) #> Variance (MLE):                                      (0.0070464, 0.020885) #> Unbiased variance:                                                       - #> AIC for the distribution of N:                                           - #>  #> --- Data Summary --- #>                               Value #> Current population size, nq:     47 #> xd = ln(nq / ne):            1.5476 #> Sample size, q + 1:              29 #>  #> --- Input Parameters --- #>                                                         Parameter #> Time unit:                                                  years #> Extinction threshold of population size, ne:                   10 #> Time window for extinction risk evaluation (years), th:       100 #> Significance level, alpha:                                   0.05 ext_di(dat, th = 100, ne = 10, qq_plot = TRUE) #> --- Estimates --- #>                                                       Estimate #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  0.096852 #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   0.0023556 #> Variance (MLE):                                        0.01087 #> Unbiased variance:                                    0.011273 #> AIC for the distribution of N:                          165.06 #>                                                                         CI #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  (1.0698e-05, 0.9898) #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   (-0.038814, 0.043525) #> Variance (MLE):                                      (0.0070464, 0.020885) #> Unbiased variance:                                                       - what: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
title: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
text: Calculates confidence interval extinction probability density-independent population model using \(w\)–\(z\) method (Hakoyama, 2025).

title: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
code: confidence_interval_wz_di(mu, xd, s, th, tq, qq, alpha)

title: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
text: mu numeric: Estimated population growth rate, \(\hat{\mu}\). xd numeric: Distance extinction threshold log scale, \(x_d = \log(n_q / n_e)\). s numeric: Estimated environmental variance, \(\hat{\sigma}^2\). th numeric: Time horizon extinction probability evaluation, \(t_h\). tq numeric: Total observation period (first last data point), \(t_q\). qq integer: Number time intervals (sample size minus 1), \(q\). alpha numeric: Significance level, .e., \(\alpha\) = 1 - (confidence level).

title: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
text: numeric vector length two containing lower upper confidence limits extinction probability.

title: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
text: improve extinction probability estimation, two transformed parameters, \(w\) \(z\), introduced functions growth rate \(\mu\) environmental variance \(\sigma^2\): $$ w = \frac{\mu t + x_d}{\sigma \sqrt{t}}, \quad z = \frac{-\mu t + x_d}{\sigma \sqrt{t}}, $$ \(t\) time horizon \(x_d = \log(n_q / n_e)\) log-distance extinction threshold. extinction probability within \(t\) expressed : $$ \Pr[T \leq t] = G(w, z) = \Phi(-w) + \exp\left(\frac{z^2 - w^2}{2}\right) \Phi(-z), $$ \(\Phi(\cdot)\) standard normal cumulative distribution function. maximum likelihood estimators \(\widehat{w}\) \(\widehat{z}\) follow non-central \(t\)-distributions \(q-1\) degrees freedom: $$ \widehat{w} \sqrt{\frac{q-1}{q}} \sqrt{\frac{t_q}{t}} \sim t(\delta_w, q-1), \quad \widehat{z} \sqrt{\frac{q-1}{q}} \sqrt{\frac{t_q}{t}} \sim t(\delta_z, q-1), $$ drift parameters $$ \delta_w = w \sqrt{\frac{t_q}{t}}, \quad \delta_z = z \sqrt{\frac{t_q}{t}}. $$ Asymptotically exact \((1-\alpha)\) confidence intervals \(w\) \(z\) obtained numerically solving non-central \(t\) quantile equations corresponding observed statistics. confidence interval extinction probability \(G(w, z)\) approximated combining intervals $$ \left( G(\overline{\Psi_w}, \underline{\Psi_z}), \quad G(\underline{\Psi_w}, \overline{\Psi_z}) \right), $$ \(\overline{\Psi_w}\), \(\underline{\Psi_w}\), \(\overline{\Psi_z}\), \(\underline{\Psi_z}\) upper lower confidence limits \(w\) \(z\), respectively. w-z method yields exact confidence intervals \(z \gg 0\) robust approximations \(z \ll 0\).

title: Computes Confidence Intervals of Extinction Risk for a Density-Independent Population Model — confidence_interval_wz_di
text: Hiroshi Hakoyama, hiroshi.hakoyama@gmail.com

what: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
text: Estimates demographic parameters extinction probabilities specified time period based density-independent population model formulated Wiener process drift approximate population dynamics environmental fluctuations. function uses time series population sizes estimate extinction risk model applies \(w\)-\(z\) method (Hakoyama, 2025), provides accurate confidence intervals conventional approaches delta method.

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
code: ext_di(   dat,   ne = 1,   th = 100,   alpha = 0.05,   unit = "years",   qq_plot = FALSE,   formatted = TRUE )

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
text: dat data frame two columns representing time population size, respectively. column names restricted, columns must ordered time first, followed population size. Time intervals need equally spaced. ne numeric: Extinction threshold, \(n_e \geq 1\). default 1 individual. th numeric: Time horizon, \(t_h\). risk extinction \(G\) defined probability population's size drop threshold \(n_e\) within given time period \(t_h\). default 100. alpha numeric: Significance level, .e., \(\alpha\) = 1 - (confidence level). default 0.05. unit character: unit time used dataset (e.g., "years", "days", "generations"). default "years". determines time unit growth rate \(\mu\), environmental variance \(\sigma^2\), extinction probability \(G\). qq_plot logical: TRUE, shows QQ-plot \(\left(\log(n_{t_i} / n_{t_{-1}}) - \hat{\mu} \tau_i\right) / \left(\hat{\sigma} \sqrt{\tau_i}\right) \sim N(0, 1)\), \(\hat{\mu}\) \(\hat{\sigma}\) denote estimated parameters. , \((n_{t_0}, n_{t_1}, \dots, n_{t_q})\) represents time series population sizes taken second column dat. default FALSE. formatted logical: TRUE, returns user-friendly, formatted output. Otherwise, returns raw list. default TRUE.

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
text: formatted = TRUE, returns following components formatted structure. formatted = FALSE, returns list: Estimates: Growth.rate: MLE growth rate, \(\hat{\mu}\). Variance: MLE environmental variance, \(\hat{\sigma}^2\). Unbiased.variance: Unbiased estimate environmental variance. AIC: Akaike Information Criterion distribution population size \(N\). Extinction.probability: MLE extinction probability within time horizon \(t_h\), denoted \(G\). lower_cl_mu: Lower \((1 - \alpha)\%\) confidence limit \(\mu\). upper_cl_mu: Upper \((1 - \alpha)\%\) confidence limit \(\mu\). lower_cl_s: Lower \((1 - \alpha)\%\) confidence limit \(\sigma^2\). upper_cl_s: Upper \((1 - \alpha)\%\) confidence limit \(\sigma^2\). lower_cl_p: Lower \((1 - \alpha)\%\) confidence limit extinction probability \(G\). upper_cl_p: Upper \((1 - \alpha)\%\) confidence limit extinction probability \(G\). Data summary: nq: Latest observed population size, \(n_q\). xd: Log-distance latest size extinction threshold, \(x_d = \log(n_q / n_e)\). sample.size: Sample size (number observations), \(q + 1\). Input parameters: unit, ne, th, alpha.

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
text: ext_di() function estimates demographic parameters extinction probabilities specified time period. based Wiener drift process model, one simplest frameworks assessing extinction risk (Lande & Orzack, 1988). Population dynamics modeled stochastic differential equation: $$ dX = \mu \, dt + \sigma \, dW, $$ \(X(t) = \log{N(t)}\) logarithm population size time \(t\), \(\mu\) growth rate, \(\sigma^2\) environmental variance, \(W(t)\) Wiener process. diffusion term right-hand side interpreted Ito sense. function implements novel method (\(w\)–\(z\) method, Hakoyama, 2025) provides accurate confidence intervals extinction risk conventional methods delta method. estimation process involves three main steps: Estimating demographic parameters population growth rate environmental variance (Dennis et al., 1991). Estimating extinction probability using analytical formulas based Wiener drift process developed Lande Orzack (1988), modeled extinction risk environmental fluctuations. Calculating confidence intervals estimates using novel method (Hakoyama, 2025). step 3, function implements novel method (\(w\)–\(z\) method, Hakoyama, 2025) introduces two transformed parameters, \(w\) \(z\), functions \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), time horizon \(t_h\), log-distance extinction threshold \(x_d\). transformations simplify extinction probability formula \(G(w, z)\) yield confidence intervals improved coverage properties compared existing methods. Key features: Accepts time series population sizes (\(n_{t_0}, n_{t_1}, \dots, n_{t_q}\)). Allows irregular measurement intervals (\(\tau_i = t_i - t_{-1}\)), permitting missing values time series (Dennis et al., 1991). Returns maximum likelihood estimates (MLEs) confidence intervals growth rate, environmental variance, extinction probability using \(w\)–\(z\) method (Hakoyama, 2025). Can generate QQ-plots assess model assumptions.

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
text: Lande, R. Orzack, S.H. (1988) Extinction dynamics age-structured populations fluctuating environment. Proceedings National Academy Sciences, 85(19), 7418–7421. Dennis, B., Munholland, P.L., Scott, J.M. (1991) Estimation growth extinction parameters endangered species. Ecological Monographs, 61, 115–143. Hakoyama, H. (2025) Confidence interval extinction risk: Revisiting misconception interval imprecise useful. Preprint, available https://###/###.pdf (accessed 2025-07-##).

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di
text: Hiroshi Hakoyama, hiroshi.hakoyama@gmail.com

title: Extinction Risk Estimation for a Density-Independent Population Model — ext_di

#> AIC for the distribution of N:                                          - #>  #> --- Data Summary --- #>                               Value #> Current population size, nq:     47 #> xd = ln(nq / ne):            3.8501 #> Sample size, q + 1:              29 #>  #> --- Input Parameters --- #>                                                         Parameter #> Time unit:                                                  years #> Extinction threshold of population size, ne:                    1 #> Time window for extinction risk evaluation (years), th:       100 #> Significance level, alpha:                                   0.05  # Probability of decline to 10 individuals within 100 years ext_di(dat, th = 100, ne = 10) #> --- Estimates --- #>                                                       Estimate #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  0.096852 #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   0.0023556 #> Variance (MLE):                                        0.01087 #> Unbiased variance:                                    0.011273 #> AIC for the distribution of N:                          165.06 #>                                                                         CI #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  (1.0698e-05, 0.9898) #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   (-0.038814, 0.043525) #> Variance (MLE):                                      (0.0070464, 0.020885) #> Unbiased variance:                                                       - #> AIC for the distribution of N:                                           - #>  #> --- Data Summary --- #>                               Value #> Current population size, nq:     47 #> xd = ln(nq / ne):            1.5476 #> Sample size, q + 1:              29 #>  #> --- Input Parameters --- #>                                                         Parameter #> Time unit:                                                  years #> Extinction threshold of population size, ne:                   10 #> Time window for extinction risk evaluation (years), th:       100 #> Significance level, alpha:                                   0.05  # With QQ-plot ext_di(dat, th = 100, ne = 10, qq_plot = TRUE)  #> --- Estimates --- #>                                                       Estimate #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  0.096852 #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   0.0023556 #> Variance (MLE):                                        0.01087 #> Unbiased variance:                                    0.011273 #> AIC for the distribution of N:                          165.06 #>                                                                         CI #> Probability of decline to 10 within 100 years (MLE):  (1.0698e-05, 0.9898) #> Growth rate (MLE):                                   (-0.038814, 0.043525) #> Variance (MLE):                                      (0.0070464, 0.020885) #> Unbiased variance:                                                       - what: Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
title: Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
text: Computes extinction probability density-independent stochastic population model based given w z statistics.

title: Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
text: w numeric: w statistic extinction probability calculation. z numeric: z statistic extinction probability calculation.

title: Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
text: numeric: Estimated extinction probability.

title: Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
text: Let \(T\) random variable representing time population becomes extinct. extinction probability within given time horizon \(t_h\) defined \(\Pr[T \leq t_h]\). Hakoyama (2025) expressed extinction probability function two transformed statistics, \(w\) \(z\), yielding following formula: $$ \Pr[T \leq t_h] = G(w, z) = \Phi(-w) + \exp{\left(\frac{z^2 - w^2}{2}\right)} \Phi(-z), $$ \(\Phi(\cdot)\) cumulative distribution function standard normal distribution. large values \(z\) (typically \(z \geq 35\)), exact formula may suffer numerical inaccuracies. Dennis et al. (1991) derived asymptotic approximation extinction probabilities expressed directly terms \(\mu\), \(\sigma^2\), \(n_q\), \(n_e\), \(t_h\). package reformulates approximation terms \(w\) \(z\), resulting following asymptotic expansion: $$ G(w, z) \approx \Phi(-w) + \exp{\left(-\frac{w^2}{2}\right)} \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \left(\frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{z^3} + \frac{3}{z^5} - \frac{15}{z^7} + \frac{105}{z^9} - \frac{945}{z^{11}} + \frac{10395}{z^{13}}\right). $$ approximation improves numerical stability maintaining high accuracy large \(z\).

title: Computes Extinction Probability for a Density-Independent Model — extinction_probability_wz_di
text: Hiroshi Hakoyama, hiroshi.hakoyama@gmail.com

title: Computes the w Statistic — w_statistic

title: Computes the w Statistic — w_statistic
text: mu numeric: Estimated population growth rate, \(\hat{\mu}\). xd numeric: Distance extinction threshold log scale, \(x_d = \log(n_q / n_e)\). s numeric: Estimated environmental variance, \(\hat{\sigma}^2\). th numeric: Time horizon extinction probability evaluation, denoted \(t_h\).

title: Computes the w Statistic — w_statistic
text: numeric: Value w statistic.

title: Computes the w Statistic — w_statistic
text: \(w\) statistic defined $$ w = \frac{\mu t_h + x_d}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 t_h}}, $$ \(\mu\) population growth rate, \(\sigma^2\) environmental variance, \(t_h\) time horizon, \(x_d\) log-distance extinction threshold.

title: Computes the z Statistic — z_statistic
